text: Risk sentiment rebounded sharply on June 18, 2026: the S&P 500 rose 80.48 points, or 1.08%, to 7,500.58, erasing most of the prior session's 1.21% drop as geopolitical de-escalation and a chip-sector rally offset the hawkish Federal Reserve signal from June 17.

Growth and tech stocks led the rebound: the Nasdaq Composite surged 496.28 points, or 1.91%, to 26,517.93, the strongest advance among the major indexes, powered by a 6.4% rally in the Philadelphia semiconductor index after Intel shares jumped 10.6% to a record high on news of an Apple chip-design partnership.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 72.15 points, or 0.14%, to 51,564.70, a comparatively muted advance that reflected the day's risk rotation into growth and semis rather than blue chips, with energy stocks dragging as oil prices fell on the U.S.-Iran peace agreement.

Small caps outperformed large caps: the Russell 2000 jumped 2.12% to 2,979.77, the best-performing major index on the day, as a modest decline in Treasury yields eased funding-cost pressure on rate-sensitive smaller companies even amid the Fed's hawkish tilt.

The Cboe Volatility Index plunged 2.04 points, or 11.06%, to close at 16.40 on June 18, 2026, unwinding nearly all of the prior session's 12.37% spike to 18.44 and signaling that the post-FOMC hedging bid dissipated as geopolitical risk premia collapsed on the U.S.-Iran peace deal.

Breadth was mixed and narrower than the headline rally suggests: five of 11 major S&P 500 industry sectors finished higher, with technology leading gainers while energy lagged as oil prices slid, contrasting with the prior session's uniformly negative all-11-red breadth.

The Philadelphia semiconductor index soared 6.4%, sharply outperforming the broad market, after President Trump said Apple had agreed to work with Intel to design and manufacture chips in the U.S., sending Intel shares up 10.6% to a record close and lifting the entire chip complex including SanDisk (+11.6%) and Super Micro (+10.1%).

The CNN Fear & Greed Index read 38 ('Fear') as of the June 18 close, up from 32 the previous day and 31 one week earlier, reflecting a market still in fear territory despite the day's rally — the index had not yet recovered to its prior-month reading of 61 ('Greed'), indicating that the rebound did not fully erase the sentiment damage from the Fed-driven sell-off.

Treasury yields eased across the curve: the 2-year note finished at 4.19% and the 10-year at 4.46%, down from the prior session's post-FOMC spike (when the 2-year had jumped to 4.21%), as the geopolitical risk premium embedded in oil prices unwound, pulling real yields lower and supporting the risk-on tone in equities.

Positioning confirmed the Fed's hawkish signal remained priced even after the rebound: CME Group's FedWatch tool showed traders assigning roughly a 50% probability to a 25-basis-point rate hike by September and about a 20% probability to a 50-basis-point hike, holding firm from the prior session's post-FOMC reprice even as risk assets rallied.

subject.name: June 18 sentiment driver
subject.event: US-Iran peace deal, chip rally, and Fed hawkish repricing

text: The mood shift had three reinforcing engines: first, the formal signing of the U.S.-Iran peace agreement at the Palace of Versailles and the lifting of the U.S. naval blockade on the Strait of Hormuz, which sent oil prices to their lowest since early March and eased inflation fears; second, a chip-sector surge after Trump announced an Apple-Intel manufacturing partnership that drove the PHLX semiconductor index up 6.4%; and third, softer Treasury yields that supported small caps and rate-sensitive equities, with SignatureFD CIO Tony Welch noting the 'package of data is still supportive whether or not the Fed has become a little bit more hawkish,' even as Cantor's Eric Johnston concluded 'the Fed has more credibility around inflation.'
